title: Order of Fields in an Entity
breadcrumbs: Order of Fields in an Entity
text: Order of Fields in an Entity The order of fields in an entity influences performance because of a Uniface feature called field sub-setting, in which Uniface groups the fields in an occurrence into subsets of adjacent fields and then either formats these as groups or not. Field sub-setting is part of the formatting process applied to the contents of component fields when displayed or referenced in ProcScript. Uniface creates subsets so that four or more adjacent fields that are not used (and therefore need no formatting) are grouped together and not formatted. As a general rule, the most frequently used fields should form contiguous groups within a row. Another reason for ordering fields within a row only applies to non-relational databases, such as flat file systems. For those databases, each row is read until the field or fields needed are found, so the most frequently used fields should be placed at the beginning of a row. The field U_VERSION plays a particular role in this context. If defined, Uniface uses U_VERSION for all three locking strategies to check if an occurrence in the database has changed, instead of going through all the individual fields of a row. U_VERSION should therefore be placed directly behind the primary key fields in a row. In summary, the suggested order of fields is: Primary key fields (contiguous) U_VERSION (if used) Foreign key fields Candidate keys Indexed fields Most commonly used fixed length fields Least commonly used fixed length fields Variable length fields For example, entity A combines two entities (B and C) with a many-to-many relationship. Entity B most often serves as entry point of an access path. In this case, the foreign key that implements the relationship to entity B should be placed before the foreign key to entity C. Related Links U_VERSION for Improving Locking Performance Uniface supports a version mechanism which can improve performance by using a field called U_VERSION. U_VERSION is a one-byte field which you must assign to an entity for it to work. In other words, it is not automatically available. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
